ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for karaage prawn roll; mapped to closest parent class 'fried shrimp' (FOODON_00002047). A more specific term for karaage-style fried seafood roll does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, fried) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for karaage-style prawn rolls with yuzu mayo sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for restaurant-style fried prawn roll preparations.

serving.description: 1 serving of prawn rolls with sauces
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of approximately 200 g, comprising several deep-fried prawn rolls accompanied by yuzu-flavoured mayonnaise dipping sauce, as typically portioned at a Japanese restaurant or food-delivery outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, coated, fried (SR Legacy ID 15152) combined with mayonnaise and yuzu sauce component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for karaage-style prawn roll with yuzu mayo exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for fried coated shrimp (FDC ID 175177 / SR Legacy 15152) as the primary anchor, with mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741) and yuzu citrus juice component adjustments. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, and saturated fat are cross-checked against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and retained where consistent with the USDA fried shrimp analogue. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdowns for this composite dish are not available in any approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on battered fried fish/seafood (GI ~55, low-medium boundary)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for karaage-style prawn rolls with yuzu mayo. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food category: battered and deep-fried seafood with wheat flour coating, as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as 'low'. The wheat flour batter is the primary glycaemic driver; prawn protein and fat slow gastric emptying, moderating the glycaemic response. GL per serving = GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 55 × (12.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 55 × 25 / 100 = 13.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 200 g serving delivers 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce, salt, and mayonnaise; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤100 g) and avoid additional high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55) with moderate glycaemic load (GL 13.75 per 200 g serving) driven by wheat flour batter; protein (12.5 g/100 g) and fat (16 g/100 g) slow postprandial glucose rise, but deep-frying increases caloric density.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a small portion (≤100 g) occasionally, pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, while monitoring total carbohydrate intake from the wheat flour batter and any accompanying rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (425 mg/100 g) raise cardiovascular risk; cholesterol at 90 mg/100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g add further concern for individuals with existing CVD.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried dish due to its combined burden of total fat, sodium, cholesterol, and trans fat; grilled or steamed prawn alternatives are strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium at 425 mg/100 g also exceeds the RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to AGE formation from deep-frying and high sodium content; request steamed or grilled prawn preparations instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Roll Mayo Karaage with Yuzu is a Japanese-inspired deep-fried dish served at Japanese restaurants and food-delivery outlets in Singapore. The dish consists of prawn rolls coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried karaage-style until golden and crispy, then served with a creamy yuzu-infused mayonnaise dipping sauce. Karaage (唐揚げ) is a Japanese frying technique in which protein is marinated — typically in soy sauce, ginger, and sake — before being lightly coated in potato starch or flour and fried at high temperature to produce a light, crunchy exterior with a juicy interior. The yuzu (柚子) citrus element adds a bright, floral tartness that cuts through the richness of the mayonnaise and the oil from frying. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 240 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 16 g fat, 12.5 g carbohydrate, and 425 mg sodium. The high sodium content (425 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis. The low GI (55) with moderate glycaemic load (GL ~13.75 per 200 g serving) means people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size. The dish contains crustacean, gluten, egg, and soy allergens. It is not vegetarian, vegan, or certified halal. Clinically, the deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and relevant for patients with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease. The yuzu component contributes a small amount of vitamin C (2.5 mg/100 g). This dish is typically consumed as a starter, side dish, or snack within a Japanese meal context.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for karaage-style prawn roll with yuzu mayo exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried coated shrimp as the closest approved analogue, cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried seafood data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fried coated shrimp, FDC ID 175177) as the closest approved analogue; no exact match exists in any approved source for this specific dish. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with the USDA analogue. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profiles, full mineral panel, and moisture data for this composite deep-fried prawn roll dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or any other approved source as of 2024. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried seafood from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as 'low'; gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. GL per 200 g serving = 55 × (12.5 × 2) / 100 = 13.75. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish may contain mirin or sake in the marinade, which would render it non-halal. 5. The dish is sold under the menu code '707D' at a GrabFood-listed Japanese outlet in Singapore; the canonical food name has been normalised to remove the menu code prefix. 6. FoodOn match is approximate (deep-fried food product parent class); no species- and preparation-specific FoodOn term exists for karaage prawn rolls. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, fried) is the closest defensible match.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Updated ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' as per G5 feedback. Added Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category to 'medium' for GI 55 — subsequently identified as incorrect per University of Sydney GI Database classification.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with GI value of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect correct 'low GI' classification. Updated notes section to document the correction.
